**Which relationship configuration will achieve this requirement while maintaining optimal query performance?**

options.A: Create active **many-to-one** relationships: OrderDetails→Products, OrderDetails→Orders, Products→Categories, Orders→Customers, Customers→Regions. Use **single cross-filter direction** on all relationships.
options.B: Create active **many-to-one** relationships: OrderDetails→Products, OrderDetails→Orders, Products→Categories, Orders→Customers, Customers→Regions. Use **both cross-filter directions** on all relationships.
options.C: Create **one-to-many** relationships from each dimension to each fact table directly: Categories→OrderDetails, Regions→OrderDetails, Customers→OrderDetails. Mark all as active with **both cross-filter directions**.
options.D: Create active **many-to-many** relationships between OrderDetails and Products, and between Orders and Customers. Use **single cross-filter direction** and a bridge table for Categories and Regions.

explanation:
**Why Option A is correct:**

**1. Correct Cardinality (Many-to-One):**
- **OrderDetails→Products** (many-to-one): Multiple line items reference one product.
- **OrderDetails→Orders** (many-to-one): Multiple line items belong to one order.
- **Products→Categories** (many-to-one): Multiple products belong to one category.
- **Orders→Customers** (many-to-one): Multiple orders belong to one customer.
- **Customers→Regions** (many-to-one): Multiple customers belong to one region.

**2. Single Cross-Filter Direction:**
Filters flow **from dimension tables toward fact tables** (Categories → Products → OrderDetails). This is the *star/snowflake schema* best practice and prevents ambiguous bidirectional propagation.

**3. All Relationships Active:**
All five relationships are active, enabling slicers on Categories, Regions, Products, and Customers to filter OrderDetails and Orders without requiring **USERELATIONSHIP()** DAX workarounds.

**Why other options are incorrect:**

**Option B — FALSE:** Using **both cross-filter directions** on all relationships creates circular or ambiguous filter paths. For example, a filter on Categories could propagate back through Products into OrderDetails and then into Orders, causing unpredictable slicer behavior and **performance degradation**.

**Option C — FALSE:** Creating direct relationships from each dimension to the fact table (bypassing the snowflake hierarchy) destroys the dimensional hierarchy. Slicing by Category would not automatically filter Products, breaking the data model's integrity and report usability.

**Option D — FALSE:** Using **many-to-many** relationships when the actual cardinality is many-to-one introduces unnecessary complexity, potential **cross-join** performance issues, and is architecturally incorrect. Many-to-many is only appropriate when neither side of the relationship has unique keys relative to the other.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationships – One-to-Many, Many-to-Many, One-to-One

text:
You are designing a **Power BI data model** for a logistics company that tracks shipments across multiple warehouses. The source data includes:

• **Shipments** table: ShipmentID, ProductID, WarehouseID, Quantity, ShipmentCost, ShipDate
• **Product** table: ProductID, ProductName, Category, UnitWeight
• **Warehouse** table: WarehouseID, WarehouseName, Region
• **Driver** table: DriverID, DriverName, ManagerID, RegionID *(ManagerID references DriverID — representing a self-referencing parent-child hierarchy)*
• **Shipment-Driver Bridge** table: ShipmentID, DriverID *(a single shipment can involve multiple drivers; a driver can handle many shipments)*

You need the model to support:
1. Filtering shipment costs by product category and warehouse region
2. Analyzing shipment performance by individual driver **and** by manager (parent-child hierarchy) with full drill-down capability in visuals
3. Avoiding duplicate aggregation of ShipmentCost

**Which relationship and calculation configuration correctly satisfies all requirements in Power BI?**

options.A: Create one-to-many relationships: Shipments→Product, Shipments→Warehouse, Shipments→Shipment-Driver Bridge, Shipment-Driver Bridge→Driver. For the manager hierarchy, create a self-referencing relationship from Driver[ManagerID] to Driver[DriverID] in the Manage Relationships dialog — this alone enables drill-down hierarchy navigation in Power BI visuals.
options.B: Create one-to-many relationships: Shipments→Product, Shipments→Warehouse, Shipments→Shipment-Driver Bridge, Shipment-Driver Bridge→Driver. For the manager hierarchy, add calculated columns in the Driver table using **DAX PATH** and **PATHITEM** functions to flatten the parent-child hierarchy, then build a navigable hierarchy from those columns.
options.C: Create one-to-many relationships: Shipments→Product, Shipments→Warehouse. Create a direct many-to-many relationship between Shipments and Driver (without the Bridge table) using the built-in many-to-many cardinality. For the manager hierarchy, use a self-referencing relationship on the Driver table to enable drill-down in visuals.
options.D: Create one-to-many relationships: Shipments→Product, Shipments→Warehouse, Shipments→Driver (direct, by adding DriverID to Shipments). Create a one-to-one relationship between Shipment-Driver Bridge and Driver. Use **DAX PATH** functions in the Driver table to flatten the manager hierarchy.

explanation:
**Correct Answer — B: Bridge Table Pattern with DAX PATH Hierarchy Flattening**

**Statement 1 — Why B is correct: Bridge Table Handles Many-to-Many — TRUE**

Because multiple drivers can handle one shipment and one driver handles many shipments, a **many-to-many** relationship exists between Shipments and Driver. The correct Power BI approach uses the **Shipment-Driver Bridge** table as a junction:

• **Shipments → Shipment-Driver Bridge** (one-to-many on ShipmentID)
• **Shipment-Driver Bridge → Driver** (many-to-one on DriverID)

This ensures ShipmentCost is never duplicated during aggregation and filters propagate correctly from Driver through Bridge to Shipments.

**Statement 2 — DAX PATH Functions Are Required for Navigable Parent-Child Hierarchies — TRUE**

While Power BI Desktop **does technically allow** self-referencing relationships (e.g., Driver[ManagerID] → Driver[DriverID]) in the Manage Relationships dialog without producing an error, such a relationship **does not enable drill-down hierarchy navigation in Power BI visuals**. A self-referencing relationship cannot be used to build a navigable hierarchy that report consumers can drill through level by level.

The correct and fully functional approach for parent-child hierarchies requiring visual drill-down is to use **DAX calculated columns** in the Driver table:

• `Path = PATH(Driver[DriverID], Driver[ManagerID])` — builds the ancestry path string
• `Level1 = PATHITEM([Path], 1, INTEGER)`, `Level2 = PATHITEM([Path], 2, INTEGER)`, etc. — flattens each level

These flattened columns are then assembled into a **Power BI hierarchy**, enabling full drill-down from top-level manager to individual driver in any visual.

**Why Other Options Fail:**

**Statement 3 — Why A is incorrect: Self-Referencing Relationship Does Not Enable Visual Drill-Down — TRUE**

Option A correctly uses the bridge table pattern but incorrectly claims that a self-referencing relationship from Driver[ManagerID] to Driver[DriverID] alone enables drill-down hierarchy navigation in Power BI visuals. Although Power BI Desktop permits creating this self-referencing relationship without error, it **cannot be used to construct a navigable hierarchy** for drill-down in reports. Without the DAX PATH/PATHITEM flattening approach, managers and their subordinates cannot be explored hierarchically in visuals. Option A therefore fails requirement 2.

**Statement 4 — Why C is incorrect: Built-in Many-to-Many Without Bridge Risks Ambiguous Aggregation — TRUE**

Option C removes the Bridge table and uses Power BI's built-in **many-to-many cardinality** directly between Shipments and Driver. While Power BI technically supports this, it:

• Can produce **ambiguous filter propagation** and unexpected cross-filter behavior
• Does not correctly represent the intermediate assignment data held in the Bridge table
• Also relies on a self-referencing relationship for hierarchy navigation, which — as explained above — does not support visual drill-down

The bridge table pattern is the *recommended best practice* for many-to-many scenarios because it preserves assignment granularity and keeps aggregations unambiguous.

**Statement 5 — Why D is incorrect: Direct Join Breaks Many-to-Many; One-to-One Cardinality Is Wrong — TRUE**

Option D adds DriverID directly to Shipments, assuming one driver per shipment — this **loses the many-to-many business requirement**. Additionally, creating a one-to-one relationship between the Bridge table and Driver is incorrect because the Bridge table contains many rows per DriverID (the same driver across multiple shipments), making the cardinality inherently many-to-one, not one-to-one. Power BI will raise a cardinality warning or produce incorrect results.

**Key Principle:**

For many-to-many scenarios, use a **junction/bridge table** with two one-to-many relationships. For parent-child hierarchies requiring visual drill-down in Power BI, always use **DAX PATH, PATHITEM, and PATHLENGTH** calculated columns to flatten the hierarchy into explicit level columns — a self-referencing table relationship, while permitted, does not support navigable hierarchy drill-down in visuals.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationships – One-to-Many, Many-to-Many, One-to-One

text:
You are designing a **Power BI data model** for a retail analytics solution. Your source data contains three tables:

• **Products**: ProductID (unique), ProductName, CategoryID, UnitPrice
• **Categories**: CategoryID (unique), CategoryName, DepartmentID
• **Sales**: SalesID (unique), ProductID, Quantity, SaleDate, StoreID

During model review, you discover that:
- A single product can appear in multiple sales transactions
- A single category contains many products; each product belongs to exactly one category
- Store information is currently embedded in the Sales table rather than normalized into a separate table

Your manager asks you to identify the **optimal relationship configuration** that:
1. Correctly reflects actual data cardinality
2. Allows dimension tables to filter the Sales fact table
3. Avoids **ambiguous filter paths** and **circular dependencies**
4. Supports accurate aggregations such as total sales by category and sales count by store

In the options below, **(1→*)** denotes a **One-to-Many relationship with single cross-filter direction** from the one-side to the many-side. **(1↔*)** denotes the same cardinality but with **bidirectional cross-filter direction**.

Which relationship configuration is **most appropriate** for this scenario?

options.A: Products **(1↔*)** Sales; Categories **(1↔*)** Products; normalize Stores in a separate table with **(1↔*)** relationship to Sales
options.B: Products **(1→*)** Sales; Categories **(1→*)** Products; embed Stores as a denormalized column in Sales and use a One-to-One relationship to a Stores lookup table
options.C: Products **(1→*)** Sales; Categories **(*↔*)** Products via a bridge table; normalize Stores in a separate table with **(1→*)** relationship to Sales
options.D: Products **(1→*)** Sales; Categories **(1→*)** Products; normalize Stores in a separate table with **(1→*)** relationship to Sales

explanation:
**Correct Answer — D**

This configuration correctly implements **best-practice star schema design** for the described scenario:

**Products (1→*) Sales**: One-to-Many with single cross-filter direction from Products to Sales. Each product can appear in many sales; each sale references exactly one product. Single direction ensures filters on Products propagate cleanly to Sales without creating circular paths.

**Categories (1→*) Products**: One-to-Many from Categories to Products. Each category contains many products, and each product belongs to exactly one category — consistent with the source data. This maintains a proper **dimensional hierarchy** without ambiguity.

**Stores (normalized, 1→* to Sales)**: Removing embedded store data from Sales creates a proper **conformed dimension**. One-to-Many cardinality is correct (one store → many sales), and single cross-filter direction ensures store-level filtering of sales measures is predictable and unambiguous.

---

**Why other options fail:**

**Option A — INCORRECT**: Using **bidirectional cross-filter direction (1↔*)** on all three relationships creates **ambiguous filter paths**. When multiple bidirectional relationships exist in a chain (Categories ↔ Products ↔ Sales ↔ Stores), Power BI can produce unexpected results because filters may propagate in unintended directions, leading to measure errors in aggregations.

**Option B — INCORRECT**: Embedding Stores in the Sales fact table violates **data normalization principles**, introducing redundancy and making it impossible to manage store attributes independently. A One-to-One relationship between duplicated embedded data and a lookup table creates a **redundant key structure** that does not solve the underlying design problem.

**Option C — INCORRECT**: Applying a **Many-to-Many (\*↔\*) relationship** between Categories and Products contradicts the stated fact that each product belongs to exactly one category (CategoryID is a single FK on Products). This configuration introduces unnecessary complexity and incorrect cardinality, leading to **measure duplication errors**.

---

**Why D is optimal:**

1. **Correct cardinality**: All relationships match actual data (1→* throughout).
2. **Unambiguous filter propagation**: Single cross-filter direction from each dimension to the fact table prevents circular or unintended filter paths.
3. **Normalized dimensions**: Stores becomes a proper **conformed dimension**, enabling accurate sales count by store.
4. **Measure integrity**: Totals by category and counts by store calculate correctly because the filter chain Categories→Products→Sales is linear and deterministic.
5. **Scalability**: The *Kimball star schema* pattern allows additional fact tables to be added without **cardinality conflicts**.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationships – One-to-Many, Many-to-Many, One-to-One

text:
You are building a **Power BI data model** for a manufacturing company. The following tables exist in the model:

- **Employees** (EmployeeID, FullName, DepartmentID, JobTitle)
- **Departments** (DepartmentID, DepartmentName, LocationID)
- **Locations** (LocationID, City, Country)
- **Projects** (ProjectID, ProjectName, LeadEmployeeID, DepartmentID)
- **ProjectAssignments** (AssignmentID, ProjectID, EmployeeID, HoursLogged)

The business requires:
1. Filtering reports by **Department** and **Location**
2. Reporting **total hours logged per employee per project**
3. Identifying the **lead employee** for each project
4. Analyzing **which employees work on which projects** (many employees per project; many projects per employee)

You must configure relationships to meet all four requirements. Which relationship configuration is **most appropriate**?

options.A: Create one-to-many from Departments to Employees, one-to-many from Locations to Departments, one-to-many from Employees to ProjectAssignments, one-to-many from Projects to ProjectAssignments, and one-to-many from Departments to Projects. Set the Employees-to-Projects relationship through LeadEmployeeID as an additional active relationship.
options.B: Create one-to-many from Departments to Employees, one-to-many from Locations to Departments, one-to-many from Employees to ProjectAssignments, one-to-many from Projects to ProjectAssignments, and one-to-many from Departments to Projects. Add a separate **inactive** relationship from Employees (EmployeeID) to Projects (LeadEmployeeID), activated via **USERELATIONSHIP** in DAX measures when identifying the project lead.
options.C: Create a many-to-many relationship directly between Employees and Projects to handle requirement 4, one-to-many from Departments to Employees, and one-to-many from Locations to Departments. Skip the ProjectAssignments table as it introduces unnecessary complexity.
options.D: Create one-to-many from Departments to Employees, one-to-many from Locations to Departments, one-to-many from Projects to ProjectAssignments, and one-to-many from Employees to ProjectAssignments. Set both the Departments-to-Projects and Employees-to-Projects (LeadEmployeeID) relationships as active to avoid using DAX.

explanation:
**Explanation**

**Why Option B is correct:**

**Requirement 1 — Filter by Department and Location:** One-to-many from Locations → Departments → Employees forms a clean filter chain. One-to-many from Departments → Projects allows department-level project filtering.

**Requirement 2 — Hours logged per employee per project:** The **ProjectAssignments** table acts as a **bridge (associative) table** resolving the many-to-many between Employees and Projects. One-to-many from Employees → ProjectAssignments and one-to-many from Projects → ProjectAssignments enables aggregation of HoursLogged at any granularity.

**Requirement 3 — Lead employee per project:** Projects.LeadEmployeeID is a **role-playing relationship** — a second relationship from Employees.EmployeeID to Projects.LeadEmployeeID. Because Employees already has an active relationship to ProjectAssignments, a **second active relationship to Projects would create ambiguous filter paths**. The correct approach is to mark this as **inactive** and activate it only in specific DAX measures using **USERELATIONSHIP**, for example:
`CALCULATE([Measure], USERELATIONSHIP(Employees[EmployeeID], Projects[LeadEmployeeID]))`

**Requirement 4 — Many employees per project, many projects per employee:** This is correctly resolved through **ProjectAssignments** as the junction table, avoiding a native many-to-many relationship (which can introduce unexpected cross-filter behaviour and performance issues).

**Why the other options fail:**

**Option A — FALSE:** Adding a *second active relationship* from Employees to Projects (via LeadEmployeeID) while an active path already exists through ProjectAssignments creates **ambiguous filter propagation**. **Power BI prohibits two active relationships between the same pair of tables**.

**Option C — FALSE:** Creating a native **many-to-many** directly between Employees and Projects discards the HoursLogged grain stored in ProjectAssignments, making requirement 2 impossible. Skipping the bridge table is an anti-pattern when transactional data exists at the assignment level.

**Option D — FALSE:** Attempting to set both Departments-to-Projects and the role-playing Employees-to-Projects (LeadEmployeeID) as active simultaneously creates **multiple active paths** between Employees and Projects, which **Power BI will reject or produce ambiguous results**. Avoiding **USERELATIONSHIP** here is not a valid justification for making conflicting relationships active.

**Key Principles Applied:**
- *Role-playing dimensions* require inactive relationships activated via **USERELATIONSHIP**
- *Many-to-many* business relationships should be resolved through a **bridge/junction table** at the correct grain
- **Power BI enforces that only one active relationship can exist between any two tables**
- Filter chain integrity depends on correct cardinality direction in all one-to-many relationships

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationships – One-to-Many, Many-to-Many, One-to-One

text:
A retail company has two tables in their **Power BI** data model:

- **Sales**: Contains one row per transaction, with columns `TransactionID`, `StoreID`, `ProductID`, and `Revenue`.
- **StoreProduct**: A bridge table containing combinations of `StoreID` and `ProductID` that represent which products are sold in which stores.

The data analyst needs to relate **Sales** to **StoreProduct** so that report visuals can correctly filter sales by both store and product simultaneously. The analyst notices that neither `StoreID` alone nor `ProductID` alone is unique in either table — both tables have repeating values in each of these columns individually.

Which relationship type and configuration should the analyst implement between **Sales** and **StoreProduct**?

options.A: Create a **many-to-many** relationship directly between **Sales** and **StoreProduct** using both `StoreID` and `ProductID` as a composite key by adding a calculated column concatenating the two columns in each table, then creating a **one-to-many** relationship on the new composite key column.
options.B: Create two separate **many-to-many** relationships — one between **Sales[StoreID]** and **StoreProduct[StoreID]**, and another between **Sales[ProductID]** and **StoreProduct[ProductID]** — and set both to **bidirectional** cross-filter.
options.C: Create a **one-to-one** relationship between **Sales** and **StoreProduct** using `TransactionID` as the key, since each transaction maps to exactly one store-product combination.
options.D: Create a **many-to-many** relationship directly on **Sales[StoreID]** = **StoreProduct[StoreID]** with **single-direction** cross-filter, because Power BI natively resolves composite key matching at query time.

explanation:
**Option A — CORRECT:** When neither column alone is unique in either table, a *composite key* approach is required. By concatenating `StoreID` and `ProductID` into a single calculated column (e.g., `StoreID & "-" & ProductID`) in both **Sales** and **StoreProduct**, each row in **StoreProduct** can become unique (since it represents distinct store-product pairs), enabling a **one-to-many** relationship from **StoreProduct** (one side, unique composite key) to **Sales** (many side). This is the standard Power BI best-practice pattern for composite key relationships.

**Option B — FALSE:** Creating two independent **many-to-many** relationships on individual columns does not enforce the combined store-product logic. Power BI would evaluate each relationship independently, producing incorrect cross-filtering results because a sale at Store A for Product X could incorrectly match any product at Store A or any store selling Product X.

**Option C — FALSE:** A **one-to-one** relationship on `TransactionID` would not connect the tables on `StoreID` or `ProductID` at all, making it impossible to filter sales by store-product combinations. Additionally, `TransactionID` does not exist in **StoreProduct**.

**Option D — FALSE:** **Power BI does not support native composite key matching** in relationships. A relationship defined only on `StoreID` between the two tables would ignore `ProductID` entirely, leading to incorrect aggregations and filter propagation. Many-to-many on a single non-unique column without a proper composite key structure produces ambiguous results.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationships – One-to-Many, Many-to-Many, One-to-One

text:
You are designing a Power BI data model for a consulting firm. The model contains the following tables:

- **Projects** (ProjectID, ProjectName, ClientID) — 275 rows, each row is a unique project.
- **Consultants** (ConsultantID, ConsultantName, SkillLevel) — 89 rows, each row is a unique consultant.
- **Assignments** (AssignmentID, ProjectID, ConsultantID, HoursWorked, BillingRate) — 1,730 rows; a consultant can be assigned to many projects, and a project can have many consultants.
- **Clients** (ClientID, ClientName, Region) — 47 rows, each row is a unique client.
- **ClientAccountManagers** (ClientID, ManagerName, StartDate, EndDate) — 47 rows; each client has exactly one current account manager and each manager handles exactly one client.

You need the model to correctly support the following requirements:

1. A report visual must show total **HoursWorked** per consultant, filtered by client region.
2. Filtering from **Projects** to **Consultants** (and vice versa) must propagate correctly through **Assignments**.
3. The relationship between **Clients** and **ClientAccountManagers** must enforce a strict one-to-one mapping.

Which combination of relationship configurations satisfies ALL three requirements?

options.A: **Clients** → **Projects**: One-to-Many (single direction). **Projects** → **Assignments**: One-to-Many (single direction). **Consultants** → **Assignments**: One-to-Many (single direction). **Assignments** cross-filter direction set to **Both** on the Projects–Assignments relationship. **Clients** → **ClientAccountManagers**: One-to-One (single direction).
options.B: **Clients** → **Projects**: One-to-Many (single direction). **Projects** → **Assignments**: One-to-Many (single direction). **Consultants** → **Assignments**: One-to-Many (single direction). Cross-filter direction set to **Both** on both the Projects–Assignments and Consultants–Assignments relationships. **Clients** → **ClientAccountManagers**: One-to-One (single direction).
options.C: **Clients** → **Projects**: One-to-Many (single direction). **Projects** → **Consultants**: Many-to-Many (both directions). **Clients** → **ClientAccountManagers**: One-to-One (single direction). No **Assignments** table in the model.
options.D: **Clients** → **Projects**: One-to-Many (single direction). **Projects** → **Assignments**: One-to-Many (both directions). **Consultants** → **Assignments**: One-to-Many (both directions). **Clients** → **ClientAccountManagers**: One-to-Many (single direction).

explanation:
Let's evaluate each option against the three requirements:

**Option A — INCORRECT:** It sets bi-directional cross-filtering only on the Projects–Assignments relationship. For Requirement 1 (HoursWorked per consultant filtered by client region), the filter must flow from **Clients** → **Projects** → **Assignments** → **Consultants**. With single-direction filtering on Consultants → Assignments, the filter cannot propagate *back* from Assignments to Consultants. The filter flows *from* Consultants *to* Assignments by default (one-to-many, single direction), but the requirement needs it to flow *from* Assignments *to* Consultants (reverse direction). Setting **Both** only on the Projects–Assignments relationship is insufficient — the Consultants–Assignments relationship also needs **Both** so that the chain Clients → Projects → Assignments → Consultants is complete.

**Option B — CORRECT:** This creates a proper **star-like schema** with Assignments as a bridge table. Setting cross-filter to **Both** on *both* relationships involving Assignments allows filters to propagate in both directions through the bridge: from Projects to Consultants (via Assignments) and from Consultants to Projects (via Assignments). This satisfies Requirement 2. The chain **Clients** → **Projects** → **Assignments** → **Consultants** is fully traversable, satisfying Requirement 1. The **One-to-One** relationship between Clients and ClientAccountManagers enforces the strict 1:1 mapping for Requirement 3.

**Option C — INCORRECT:** Removing the **Assignments** table eliminates the HoursWorked and BillingRate data entirely, making it impossible to report on HoursWorked per consultant. A direct **Many-to-Many** between Projects and Consultants loses the granular assignment-level measures.

**Option D — INCORRECT:** While the bi-directional filters on both Assignments relationships would handle Requirements 1 and 2, this option defines **Clients** → **ClientAccountManagers** as **One-to-Many** instead of **One-to-One**, violating Requirement 3's strict one-to-one enforcement. A One-to-Many relationship would allow multiple ClientAccountManagers rows per client, failing to enforce the 1:1 constraint at the model level.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationships – One-to-Many, Many-to-Many, One-to-One

text:
You are a Power BI data analyst at a healthcare organization. You have the following tables in your model:

- **PatientVisits** (fact table): Contains 2.7 million rows. Each row records a single visit. Columns include *VisitID*, *PatientKey*, *PrimaryDiagnosisCode*, *SecondaryDiagnosisCode*, *FacilityKey*, *VisitDate*.
- **DiagnosisCodes** (dimension table): Contains 68,500 unique diagnosis codes. Columns include *DiagnosisCode*, *DiagnosisDescription*, *DiagnosisCategory*.
- **Facilities** (dimension table): Contains 37 facilities. Columns include *FacilityKey*, *FacilityName*, *Region*.

Business requirements:
1. Users must be able to filter reports by **DiagnosisCategory** and see all visits where that category appears as *either* the primary or secondary diagnosis.
2. A single visit can have different codes in *PrimaryDiagnosisCode* and *SecondaryDiagnosisCode*.
3. The model must use **Import** storage mode and follow star-schema best practices where possible.

You create two relationships from **PatientVisits** to **DiagnosisCodes**: one on *PrimaryDiagnosisCode* and one on *SecondaryDiagnosisCode*. Only one can be active.

Which approach correctly satisfies all three requirements while minimizing model complexity?

options.A: Set the *PrimaryDiagnosisCode* relationship as active and the *SecondaryDiagnosisCode* relationship as inactive. In every measure, use **USERELATIONSHIP** to activate the secondary relationship, and combine both results with a **CALCULATE** pattern that unions the filter contexts.
options.B: Create a single **many-to-many** relationship between **PatientVisits** and **DiagnosisCodes** by building a bridge table named **VisitDiagnosis** that unpivots *PrimaryDiagnosisCode* and *SecondaryDiagnosisCode* into separate rows, each linked to *VisitID* and *DiagnosisCode*. Relate **VisitDiagnosis** to **DiagnosisCodes** (many-to-one) and **VisitDiagnosis** to **PatientVisits** (many-to-one).
options.C: Duplicate the **DiagnosisCodes** table as **DiagnosisCodes_Secondary**, create two active one-to-many relationships (one per copy), and add both tables as independent slicers so users select primary and secondary categories separately.
options.D: Merge *PrimaryDiagnosisCode* and *SecondaryDiagnosisCode* into a single concatenated column in **PatientVisits**, then create one active relationship to **DiagnosisCodes** on the concatenated column.

explanation:
**Option B — CORRECT:** The core challenge is that each visit relates to the **DiagnosisCodes** dimension through *two* foreign key columns, and users need a single filter on **DiagnosisCategory** to capture visits matching on *either* column. The bridge table approach resolves this elegantly:

1. You create a **VisitDiagnosis** bridge table by unpivoting the two diagnosis columns, producing up to two rows per visit (one for primary, one for secondary), each containing *VisitID* and *DiagnosisCode*.
2. **VisitDiagnosis** → **DiagnosisCodes** is a standard **many-to-one** relationship on *DiagnosisCode*.
3. **VisitDiagnosis** → **PatientVisits** is a **many-to-one** relationship on *VisitID*.
4. When a user filters by **DiagnosisCategory**, the filter flows: **DiagnosisCodes** → **VisitDiagnosis** → **PatientVisits**, correctly capturing all visits where the category appears in either diagnosis role.

This follows *star-schema best practices* by introducing a proper bridge pattern for the **many-to-many** logical relationship and avoids duplicating dimension tables.

**Option A — INCORRECT:** While **USERELATIONSHIP** can activate the inactive relationship, combining results from both the active and inactive relationship in *every* measure adds significant complexity and is error-prone. Every author of every measure must remember to implement the union pattern. This does not minimize model complexity.

**Option C — INCORRECT:** Duplicating the dimension table creates two independent filter contexts. Users cannot use a *single* slicer on **DiagnosisCategory** to find visits matching on either column — they would need to interact with two separate slicers, violating requirement #1.

**Option D — INCORRECT:** Concatenating two diagnosis codes into one column would not match any single *DiagnosisCode* in the dimension table, breaking the relationship entirely. This approach is fundamentally flawed.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationships – One-to-Many, Many-to-Many, One-to-One

text:
You are designing a **Power BI** data model for a large retail organization. The model includes the following tables:

- **Customers** (CustomerID, Name, Region) — 210,000 records
- **Products** (ProductID, Category, SupplierID) — 18,600 records
- **Orders** (OrderID, CustomerID, OrderDate) — 960,000 records
- **OrderLines** (OrderLineID, OrderID, ProductID, Quantity) — 3,800,000 records
- **Campaigns** (CampaignID, CampaignName, StartDate, EndDate) — 4,300 records
- **Suppliers** (SupplierID, SupplierName, Country) — 2,100 records

Business requirements reveal:
1. A single product can be included in **many** campaigns simultaneously.
2. A single campaign can cover **many** products.
3. Analysts need to slice **Orders** and **OrderLines** metrics by **Campaign** attributes.
4. When a direct many-to-many relationship is configured between **Products** and **Campaigns** using Power BI's native many-to-many cardinality with **bidirectional cross-filtering**, analysts report that filtering from **Campaigns** unexpectedly affects **Customers** aggregations, producing inflated totals.

Which modeling approach correctly resolves the ambiguous filter propagation issue while preserving accurate aggregations across all tables?

options.A: Keep the native many-to-many relationship between **Products** and **Campaigns** with bidirectional cross-filtering; add a calculated table using DAX to isolate **Campaigns** filters from **Customers**; set the **Products** → **Suppliers** relationship to bidirectional cross-filtering.
options.B: Introduce an explicit bridge table **ProductCampaigns** (ProductID, CampaignID) with a composite natural key; relate **Products** → **ProductCampaigns** as 1:many (single direction) and **Campaigns** → **ProductCampaigns** as 1:many (single direction); set **Products** → **Suppliers** as 1:many with single cross-filter direction.
options.C: Introduce an explicit bridge table **ProductCampaigns** (ProductID, CampaignID); set both relationships (**Products** → **ProductCampaigns** and **Campaigns** → **ProductCampaigns**) as many-to-many with bidirectional cross-filtering; set **Products** → **Suppliers** as 1:1.
options.D: Denormalize **Campaigns** attributes directly into the **Products** table; configure **Products** → **Campaigns** as a 1:many relationship; enable bidirectional cross-filtering between **OrderLines** and **Customers** to allow campaign-based slicing.

explanation:
**Root cause — Why native bidirectional many-to-many causes the problem: TRUE.**
When Power BI's native many-to-many cardinality is used with **bidirectional cross-filtering** between **Products** and **Campaigns**, filters propagate in both directions across all connected tables. Because **Products** is also related to **OrderLines** → **Orders** → **Customers**, a campaign filter unexpectedly flows through **Products** into the **Customers** path, inflating or distorting aggregations. This is a well-documented filter propagation side-effect of bidirectional relationships in complex models.

**Why Option B is correct:**
Introducing an explicit **bridge table** (also called a *junction table*) named **ProductCampaigns** with a composite natural key (ProductID, CampaignID) resolves the problem cleanly:
- **Products** → **ProductCampaigns**: 1:many, single cross-filter direction (Products filters ProductCampaigns).
- **Campaigns** → **ProductCampaigns**: 1:many, single cross-filter direction (Campaigns filters ProductCampaigns).
- All filter flow is **unidirectional**, meaning a campaign filter reaches only **ProductCampaigns** and does not propagate back through **Products** into **OrderLines** or **Customers** unless explicitly required by a DAX measure.
- **Products** → **Suppliers** as 1:many with single direction correctly represents that one supplier supplies many products, without creating reverse filter paths.
- A composite natural key (ProductID + CampaignID) on a junction table is **standard relational design** and fully valid; surrogate keys are optional.

**Why A is incorrect:**
Keeping native bidirectional many-to-many and attempting to use a calculated DAX table to isolate filters does not structurally prevent filter propagation — the relationship cardinality and direction remain unchanged. Adding bidirectionality to **Products** → **Suppliers** further increases the risk of unintended filter paths, worsening the problem.

**Why C is incorrect:**
Setting the relationships from the bridge table back to **Products** and **Campaigns** as many-to-many with bidirectional cross-filtering defeats the purpose of introducing the bridge table entirely. Bidirectional many-to-many relationships involving the bridge table recreate the same ambiguous filter propagation that caused the original issue. Additionally, **Products** → **Suppliers** is inherently a 1:many relationship (not 1:1), since one supplier provides many products; setting it as 1:1 is factually wrong.

**Why D is incorrect:**
Denormalizing **Campaigns** into **Products** destroys the many-to-many nature of the relationship — a product belonging to multiple campaigns would require duplicate product rows, introducing data redundancy and breaking aggregation accuracy. Enabling bidirectional cross-filtering between **OrderLines** and **Customers** is unrelated to the campaign filtering problem and introduces additional unintended filter propagation risks.

**Summary of best practice:** An explicit bridge table with composite natural keys and strictly **unidirectional** 1:many relationships on both sides is the correct, performant, and analytically accurate approach to resolving many-to-many filter ambiguity in **Power BI** data models.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationships – One-to-Many, Many-to-Many, One-to-One

text:
You are designing a Power BI data model for a consulting firm. The model contains the following tables:

- **Consultants** (ConsultantID, Name, Region) — 85 rows
- **Projects** (ProjectID, ProjectName, StartDate) — 220 rows
- **Assignments** (AssignmentID, ConsultantID, ProjectID, HoursWorked, BillingRate) — 1,450 rows
- **CertificationTypes** (CertTypeID, CertName, Vendor) — 32 rows
- **ConsultantCertifications** (ConsultantID, CertTypeID, DateEarned) — 290 rows

Each consultant can work on multiple projects, and each project can have multiple consultants. Additionally, each consultant can hold multiple certifications, and each certification type can be held by multiple consultants.

You need to build the model so that:
1. A report visual can show **total HoursWorked** sliced by both **ProjectName** and **ConsultantName** simultaneously.
2. A separate visual can show **count of certifications** per consultant, filtered by **CertName**.
3. No bidirectional cross-filtering is used.
4. The model avoids ambiguous relationship paths.

Which relationship configuration correctly achieves ALL of these requirements?

options.A: Create a **many-to-many** direct relationship between **Consultants** and **Projects** (both directions single). Create a **many-to-many** direct relationship between **Consultants** and **CertificationTypes**. Remove the **Assignments** and **ConsultantCertifications** tables.
options.B: Create a **one-to-many** from **Consultants** to **Assignments** on ConsultantID, a **one-to-many** from **Projects** to **Assignments** on ProjectID, a **one-to-many** from **Consultants** to **ConsultantCertifications** on ConsultantID, and a **one-to-many** from **CertificationTypes** to **ConsultantCertifications** on CertTypeID. All relationships use **single** cross-filter direction.
options.C: Create a **one-to-many** from **Consultants** to **Assignments** on ConsultantID with **bidirectional** cross-filtering, a **one-to-many** from **Projects** to **Assignments** on ProjectID, a **one-to-many** from **Consultants** to **ConsultantCertifications** on ConsultantID, and a **one-to-many** from **CertificationTypes** to **ConsultantCertifications** on CertTypeID.
options.D: Create a **one-to-many** from **Consultants** to **Assignments** on ConsultantID, a **one-to-many** from **Projects** to **Assignments** on ProjectID, and a **many-to-many** direct relationship between **Consultants** and **CertificationTypes**. Remove the **ConsultantCertifications** bridge table.

explanation:
This question tests the understanding of **bridge tables** (also called *fact tables* or *junction tables*) to resolve many-to-many logical relationships using only **one-to-many** physical relationships with **single** cross-filter direction.

**Option A — INCORRECT:** Removing the **Assignments** and **ConsultantCertifications** bridge tables and using direct many-to-many relationships would lose the **HoursWorked** and **BillingRate** detail data stored in **Assignments**, as well as the **DateEarned** attribute in **ConsultantCertifications**. The measure for total HoursWorked would have no source. Additionally, direct many-to-many relationships can produce unexpected aggregation behavior and are not the best practice when a bridge table with attributes exists.

**Option B — CORRECT:** This is the classic **star schema** approach using bridge/fact tables. **Consultants** (1) → **Assignments** (many) ← **Projects** (1) creates a proper resolved many-to-many pattern through the **Assignments** bridge table. Similarly, **Consultants** (1) → **ConsultantCertifications** (many) ← **CertificationTypes** (1) resolves the second many-to-many. All relationships are **single** direction — filters flow from dimension tables through the bridge tables. Both visuals work correctly: HoursWorked is sliceable by both dimensions via the Assignments table, and certification counts are filterable by CertName via the ConsultantCertifications table. No bidirectional filtering is required because each fact/bridge table sits at the many-side end of its dimension relationships.

**Option C — INCORRECT:** This uses **bidirectional** cross-filtering on the Consultants-to-Assignments relationship, which directly violates requirement 3. While it would technically work for filtering, bidirectional filtering also introduces potential **ambiguous paths** (since Consultants connects to two fact tables) and violates the stated constraints.

**Option D — INCORRECT:** Removing the **ConsultantCertifications** bridge table means losing the **DateEarned** attribute and the ability to correctly count certifications per consultant. A direct many-to-many between Consultants and CertificationTypes without the bridge cannot accurately represent which specific consultants hold which certifications when granular data is needed.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationships – One-to-Many, Many-to-Many, One-to-One

text:
You are designing a **Power BI** data model for a retail company. The model contains the following tables:

- **Sales**: one row per transaction line item (columns: `TransactionID`, `ProductID`, `StoreID`, `CustomerID`, `Amount`)
- **Product**: one row per product (`ProductID`, `ProductName`, `CategoryID`)
- **Store**: one row per store (`StoreID`, `StoreName`, `RegionID`)
- **Customer**: one row per customer (`CustomerID`, `CustomerName`, `LoyaltyTierID`)
- **LoyaltyTier**: one row per tier (`LoyaltyTierID`, `TierName`, `DiscountRate`)
- **Region**: one row per region (`RegionID`, `RegionName`)
- **Category**: one row per category (`CategoryID`, `CategoryName`)

A business analyst reports the following:

1. A single customer can shop at many stores, and a single store serves many customers.
2. Slicing a report visual by **RegionName** and **CategoryName** simultaneously returns incorrect totals — values appear inflated or blank depending on the slicer combination.
3. The model currently has a **many-to-many** relationship configured directly between **Customer** and **Store** using a bridge table with **both filter directions set to bidirectional**.
4. The analyst wants to filter **LoyaltyTier** attributes onto **Sales** facts without ambiguity.

Which combination of changes will **most effectively** resolve the inflated/blank totals AND maintain correct filtering from **LoyaltyTier** to **Sales**?

options.A: Keep the bidirectional many-to-many between **Customer** and **Store**; add a separate bidirectional relationship between **LoyaltyTier** and **Customer** so filters propagate freely across the entire model.
options.B: Replace the bidirectional many-to-many between **Customer** and **Store** with a *bridge table* approach using two single-directional one-to-many relationships (bridge → Customer and bridge → Store with filter direction from bridge outward); set the **LoyaltyTier** → **Customer** relationship to single-direction (LoyaltyTier filters Customer), and rely on the Customer → Sales one-to-many to propagate tier filters to facts.
options.C: Convert the many-to-many between **Customer** and **Store** to a one-to-one relationship by deduplicating rows in both tables; enable bidirectional cross-filtering on every relationship in the model to ensure all slicers work correctly.
options.D: Remove the direct relationship between **Customer** and **Store** entirely and resolve their association only through **Sales** (which already contains both `CustomerID` and `StoreID`); keep all dimension-to-fact relationships as single-directional one-to-many with filter direction flowing from dimensions toward **Sales**.

explanation:
**Option D — CORRECT:**

In a well-designed *star schema*, the **Sales** fact table already contains both `CustomerID` and `StoreID`, making a direct relationship between **Customer** and **Store** redundant. Introducing a separate Customer–Store relationship (especially bidirectional many-to-many) creates **ambiguous filter paths**: **Power BI** cannot determine whether a filter on Region should travel through Sales→Store→Region or through the Customer–Store bridge→Store→Region, causing inflated or blank totals. Removing the direct Customer–Store relationship eliminates the ambiguity. With single-directional one-to-many relationships from each dimension to **Sales** (including **LoyaltyTier** → **Customer** → **Sales**), filters propagate correctly and unambiguously from every dimension to the fact table without cross-filter conflicts.

**Option A — FALSE:** Adding a second bidirectional relationship (LoyaltyTier → Customer) while keeping the problematic bidirectional many-to-many compounds the ambiguous filter path problem rather than resolving it. Bidirectional cross-filtering in a complex model frequently causes circular or multiple active path errors and incorrect aggregations.

**Option B — FALSE:** While using a bridge table with one-to-many relationships is a valid pattern for true many-to-many scenarios, it is unnecessary here because the fact table (**Sales**) already acts as the natural bridge between Customer and Store. Adding an explicit bridge table duplicates this role and introduces additional complexity. Furthermore, the described filter direction (bridge outward) would still risk ambiguous paths when combined with other dimensions like Region and Category.

**Option C — FALSE:** Converting a genuine many-to-many to a one-to-one is only valid if each customer maps to exactly one store, which contradicts the stated business rule. Enabling bidirectional cross-filtering on every relationship is a **Power BI** anti-pattern that maximises the risk of ambiguous filter contexts, circular dependencies, and performance degradation — it does not fix inflated totals.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationships – One-to-Many, Many-to-Many, One-to-One

text:
You are designing a data model in Power BI with two tables: **Products** and **Sales**. The relationship is defined as many-to-one, with Sales.ProductID linking to Products.ProductID. You need to create a report where slicing by a Product attribute should filter the Sales measures in both directions—allowing users to filter sales by product AND to see which products appear in filtered sales data.

Which combination of relationship properties should you configure?

options.A: Cross-filter direction: **Both**; Relationship status: **Active**
options.B: Cross-filter direction: **Single** (from Sales to Products); Relationship status: **Active**
options.C: Cross-filter direction: **Both**; Relationship status: **Inactive**
options.D: Cross-filter direction: **Single** (from Products to Sales); Relationship status: **Inactive**

explanation:
**Statement 1 — TRUE:** A many-to-one relationship with **cross-filter direction set to Both** enables *bidirectional filtering*—filters on either table propagate to the other, allowing product attributes to filter sales and vice versa.

**Statement 2 — TRUE:** The relationship must be **Active** to apply filtering automatically. An **Inactive** relationship exists in the model but requires explicit **USERELATIONSHIP()** function calls in DAX measures to take effect.

**Why other options are incorrect:**
- **Option B:** Single direction (Sales→Products) filters sales by product choice, but does NOT filter product attributes based on sales data—it is unidirectional only.
- **Option C:** Setting Both directions on an **Inactive** relationship means filtering would require manual DAX measures; the filter would not propagate automatically in visuals.
- **Option D:** Single direction plus Inactive combines unidirectional filtering with manual activation—neither requirement is met.

**Key principle:** Use **Both** for fully interactive, mutual filtering. Use **Single** only when you need filtering in one direction only (e.g., a lookup table that affects measures but should never be filtered by those measures). Use **Active** for automatic application; use **Inactive** for multiple relationships between the same tables where you control which one activates via DAX.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationship Properties – Cross-filter Direction (Single vs Both), Active vs Inactive

text:
You are designing a data model in **Power BI** with two tables: **Products** and **Sales**. The relationship between them has a cross-filter direction set to **Single** and is marked as **Active**. The **Sales** table has a foreign key pointing to **Products**.

In which direction does filtering flow with a **Single** cross-filter direction?

options.A: Filters flow from **Products** (one side) to **Sales** (many side) only.
options.B: Filters flow from **Sales** (many side) to **Products** (one side) only.
options.C: Filters flow in both directions simultaneously.
options.D: No filtering occurs because the relationship is not bidirectional.

explanation:
**Cross-filter Direction — Single:** When a relationship is configured with **Single** cross-filter direction, filtering flows in one direction only — from the **one** side of the relationship to the **many** side. In a standard one-to-many relationship, the lookup/dimension table (one side, e.g., **Products**) filters the fact table (many side, e.g., **Sales**). This means selecting a product in a slicer will filter the rows shown in **Sales**, but activity in **Sales** does not automatically filter back to **Products**.

**Active vs Inactive:** An **Active** relationship is automatically applied in all visuals and DAX calculations without needing the USERELATIONSHIP function. An **Inactive** relationship requires explicit activation via USERELATIONSHIP in DAX.

**Why A is correct:** With Single cross-filter direction in a one-to-many relationship from Products to Sales, filters propagate from the **one** side (Products) to the **many** side (Sales).

**Why C is incorrect:** Bidirectional filtering requires setting cross-filter direction to **Both**, which carries performance and ambiguity risks and is not the default.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationship Properties - Cross-filter Direction (Single vs Both), Active vs Inactive

text:
You are configuring a relationship between a **Customers** table and an **Orders** table in Power BI. The relationship is defined on the **CustomerID** column. You need to ensure that filtering a customer in a visual will automatically filter related orders, but filtering orders should NOT automatically filter customers.

Which combination of relationship properties should you configure?

options.A: Cross-filter direction: **Both**; Relationship status: **Active**
options.B: Cross-filter direction: **Single**; Relationship status: **Active**
options.C: Cross-filter direction: **Both**; Relationship status: **Inactive**
options.D: Cross-filter direction: **Single**; Relationship status: **Inactive**

explanation:
**Statement 1 — TRUE:** Cross-filter direction **Single** means filtering flows in ONE direction only—from the "one" side (Customers) to the "many" side (Orders). This satisfies the requirement that filtering customers filters orders, but not vice versa.

**Statement 2 — TRUE:** Relationship status **Active** means this is the primary relationship used by default for cross-filtering. An active relationship automatically propagates filters across visuals.

**Statement 3 — TRUE:** Cross-filter direction **Both** would allow bi-directional filtering, which violates the requirement (filtering orders would then filter customers).

**Statement 4 — TRUE:** Relationship status **Inactive** would disable automatic filtering entirely; filters would only apply if explicitly used in **USERELATIONSHIP()** DAX functions, which contradicts the automatic filtering requirement.

**The answer is B** because **Single** cross-filter direction combined with **Active** status delivers one-way automatic filtering from Customers → Orders, exactly as specified.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationship Properties – Cross-filter Direction (Single vs Both), Active vs Inactive

text:
You are configuring a relationship between a **Sales** table and a **Product** table in Power BI Desktop. The relationship is based on ProductID. You need to ensure that when a user filters the Product table by category, the filter automatically applies to the Sales table to show only sales for products in that category.

Which combination of relationship properties should you configure?

options.A: Set the cross-filter direction to **Both** and mark the relationship as **Active**.
options.B: Set the cross-filter direction to **Single** (from Sales to Product) and mark the relationship as **Inactive**.
options.C: Set the cross-filter direction to **Single** (from Product to Sales) and mark the relationship as **Active**.
options.D: Set the cross-filter direction to **Both** and mark the relationship as **Inactive**.

explanation:
**Statement 1 — Cross-filter direction requirement: TRUE**

To filter the Product table and have that filter propagate to the Sales table, you need the cross-filter direction to flow **from Product to Sales**. This is a **Single** direction filter, not **Both**. Option A uses **Both**, which is unnecessary and can create ambiguity in complex models. Option B uses **Single** but in the wrong direction (Sales → Product).

**Statement 2 — Active relationship requirement: TRUE**

The relationship must be marked as **Active** for automatic filtering to occur. An **Active** relationship is automatically used by Power BI when evaluating DAX formulas and applying filters across tables. An **Inactive** relationship requires explicit specification using functions like `USERELATIONSHIP()` and does not apply automatic cross-filtering.

**Why not the other options:**
- **Option A**: **Both** direction filtering is inefficient and not needed; it can cause circular logic in complex models.
- **Option B**: Wrong direction (Sales → Product instead of Product → Sales) AND **Inactive** prevents automatic filtering.
- **Option D**: **Both** is unnecessary, and **Inactive** prevents automatic filtering.

**Correct answer: C** — **Single** direction (Product → Sales) with **Active** status ensures filters from Product automatically propagate to Sales.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationship Properties – Cross-filter Direction (Single vs Both), Active vs Inactive

text:
You are building a data model in Power BI with two tables: **Sales** and **Products**. The relationship is created between Sales[ProductID] and Products[ProductID]. You want to ensure that when a user selects a product in a slicer, the Sales table filters automatically, but selections in the Sales table should NOT filter the Products table in return.

Which combination of relationship properties should you configure?

options.A: Cross-filter direction: Single (one-way), and set the relationship as Active
options.B: Cross-filter direction: Both (two-way), and set the relationship as Active
options.C: Cross-filter direction: Single (one-way), and set the relationship as Inactive
options.D: Cross-filter direction: Both (two-way), and set the relationship as Inactive

explanation:
**Statement 1 — Cross-filter Direction is Single (One-way) — TRUE:** The requirement is that Products filter Sales in one direction only. This is achieved by setting cross-filter direction to **Single (one-way)**, which means filter context flows from the **Products** table (one side of the relationship) to the **Sales** table (many side), but not in reverse.

**Statement 2 — Relationship should be Active — TRUE:** An **Active** relationship means Power BI automatically applies the filter context through this relationship when values are selected. Since you want automatic filtering when a product is selected, the relationship must be **Active**. (An Inactive relationship would require explicit **USERELATIONSHIP()** function calls in DAX measures.)

**Why not B?** Cross-filter direction **Both (two-way)** would allow selections in Sales to also filter Products, which violates the requirement.

**Why not C or D?** An **Inactive** relationship would not automatically filter; you would need to manually activate it with DAX functions, which is not the intended behavior for this standard filtering scenario.

The correct answer is **Single (one-way) + Active**.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationship Properties – Cross-filter Direction (Single vs Both), Active vs Inactive

text:
You are designing a Power BI data model with three tables: **Customers**, **Orders**, and **Products**. A relationship exists between **Orders** and **Customers** with the following current configuration:

• **Relationship Direction:** Single (from Orders to Customers)
• **Active Status:** Active

A business analyst reports that when they filter by **Customer Region** on a report visual, the **Order Count** measure does NOT filter correctly. However, when they filter by **Order Date**, the measure works as expected. What is the most likely cause of this issue?

options.A: The relationship is set to Single cross-filter direction from Orders to Customers, so filters originating in the Customers table (such as Customer Region) cannot propagate to the Orders table.
options.B: The relationship is inactive; you must set it to Active to enable filtering across both tables.
options.C: The Order Count measure is using CALCULATE() without the TREATAS() function, preventing external filters from applying.
options.D: The Customers table contains duplicate key values, breaking the relationship integrity.

explanation:
**Statement 1 — TRUE:** When a relationship is configured with **Single cross-filter direction** from **Orders** to **Customers**, filters originate from the Orders side and flow toward Customers — not the other way around. This means a filter placed on **Customer Region** (which lives in the **Customers** table) cannot travel across the relationship to reduce rows in **Orders**. As a result, the **Order Count** measure remains unaffected by Customer Region selections.

**Statement 2 — TRUE:** **Order Date** resides in the **Orders** table, which is the source (filter-origin) side of the single-direction relationship. Filters applied to Order Date operate directly on Orders rows, so the measure works correctly in that context.

**Statement 3 — FALSE:** The relationship is already stated as **Active**. An inactive relationship would prevent all implicit filtering regardless of direction, requiring explicit **USERELATIONSHIP()** or **CROSSFILTER()** DAX functions to activate it.

**Statement 4 — FALSE:** While a measure using **CALCULATE()** without proper filter context could cause issues in some scenarios, the problem here is fundamentally about **cross-filter direction** — not measure syntax or table integrity.

**Correct Answer: A**

To resolve this issue, the cross-filter direction should be changed from **Single** to **Both** (bidirectional), allowing filters from the **Customers** table to propagate into the **Orders** table and enabling Customer Region to correctly filter the Order Count measure.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationship Properties – Cross-filter Direction (Single vs Both), Active vs Inactive

text: A Power BI data model contains three tables: **Sales**, **Products**, and **Returns**. Both **Sales** and **Returns** have a **Many-to-One** relationship to **Products**, with cross-filter direction set to **Single** (filtering from **Products** to each fact table). A report page uses a **Products** slicer. Which filtering behavior will occur when a user selects a product in the slicer?

options.A: Only the **Sales** table will be filtered because **Single** direction only propagates to the first related table defined in the model.
options.B: Both **Sales** and **Returns** tables will be filtered because **Single** direction on a One-to-Many relationship propagates filters from the 'one' side (**Products**) to the 'many' side.
options.C: Neither **Sales** nor **Returns** will be filtered because **Single** direction only allows the fact tables to filter the **Products** dimension.
options.D: Both **Sales** and **Returns** tables will be filtered, but only if the relationships are set to **Both** cross-filter direction.

explanation: In Power BI, **cross-filter direction Single** on a **Many-to-One** (or One-to-Many) relationship means filters flow from the 'one' side to the 'many' side — i.e., from a **dimension** table to a **fact** table. When **Products** is the 'one' side and both **Sales** and **Returns** are on the 'many' side, selecting a value in a **Products** slicer will push that filter context down to **both** fact tables simultaneously. This is the standard and recommended setup for a *star schema*. **Option A** is incorrect — Single direction propagates to **all** related many-side tables, not just the first defined. **Option C** reverses the direction; Single from the 'one' side filters the 'many' side, not the other way around. **Option D** is incorrect — cross-filter direction **Both** is needed only when you want filters to propagate *back* from the fact table to the dimension, which is not required here.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationship Properties – Cross-filter Direction (Single vs Both), Active vs Inactive

text: You have a **Power BI data model** with three tables: **Sales** (fact), **Products** (dimension), and **Regions** (dimension). The **Sales** table has foreign keys to both **Products** and **Regions**. A **Sales-to-Products** relationship is set as **Active** with **Both** cross-filter direction. A **Sales-to-Regions** relationship is also **Active** with **Both** cross-filter direction. Users report that when they filter by **Region**, the product-related visuals are being filtered unexpectedly. You want region slicers to continue filtering **Sales** data, but you want to prevent region selections from indirectly affecting **Products** visuals. Which relationship configuration achieves this goal?

options.A: Set the **Sales-to-Products** relationship to **Single** direction (from **Products** to **Sales**) and keep the **Sales-to-Regions** relationship as **Active** with **Both** direction.
options.B: Set the **Sales-to-Regions** relationship to **Inactive** and use **USERELATIONSHIP()** in all region-based measures.
options.C: Set both the **Sales-to-Products** and **Sales-to-Regions** relationships to **Both** direction, but apply a **row-level security (RLS)** filter on the **Products** table.
options.D: Keep both the **Sales-to-Products** and **Sales-to-Regions** relationships as **Active** with **Both** direction, and add a **FILTER()** function to all product visuals.

explanation:
**Statement 1 — Why A is correct — TRUE:** The root cause of unexpected cross-filtering is the **Sales-to-Products** relationship set to **Both** direction. When a **Region** is selected, it filters the **Sales** table (through Sales-to-Regions with Both direction). Because **Sales-to-Products** is also **Both** direction, the filtered **Sales** data then reverse-filters **Products**, causing product visuals to change unexpectedly. Setting **Sales-to-Products** to **Single** direction (from **Products** to **Sales**) breaks this reverse-filter chain. In a one-to-many relationship, **Single** direction always means filters flow from the dimension (Products, one-side) to the fact table (Sales, many-side). This allows product slicers to filter Sales, but prevents Sales from filtering Products. The **Sales-to-Regions** relationship can remain **Both** because Regions and Products are independent dimensions — there is no three-way filter chain problem. Region selections will correctly filter Sales, but will not propagate to Products.

**Statement 2 — Why B is incorrect — FALSE:** Making **Sales-to-Regions** **Inactive** would disable region-based filtering of **Sales** in standard visuals without using **USERELATIONSHIP()** in every measure. The requirement explicitly states that region slicers *must* continue to filter Sales data. This approach breaks the intended user experience and requires excessive workaround code.

**Statement 3 — Why C is incorrect — FALSE:** Row-level security (**RLS**) is a data-access control mechanism that restricts which *rows* a user can see — it does not control **cross-filter direction** between tables in the model. Applying **RLS** on **Products** does not prevent bidirectional filter propagation from Regions → Sales → Products in visuals, nor does it resolve the fundamental relationship configuration problem.

**Statement 4 — Why D is incorrect — FALSE:** Keeping both relationships as **Both** direction preserves the original problem: when a Region is selected, it filters Sales, which then reverse-filters Products through the **Both** direction on **Sales-to-Products**. Adding **FILTER()** functions to visuals is a workaround that masks the model design issue and does not scale to all possible product visuals and measures. Proper relationship configuration is the correct solution.

**Key concept — Cross-filter direction in one-to-many relationships:** **Single** direction means filters propagate *only* from the one-side (dimension) to the many-side (fact). **Both** direction allows bidirectional filter propagation. In this scenario, the **Sales-to-Products** relationship set to **Both** creates an unintended filter chain (Region → Sales → Products), which is broken by changing it to **Single**.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationship Properties — Cross-filter Direction (Single vs Both), Active vs Inactive

text:
You are configuring a relationship between a **Sales** table and a **Product** table in Power BI. The relationship is currently set to **Single** cross-filter direction with **Active** status. A business requirement states that you need to filter products based on sales data, but the current relationship only allows filtering from Product to Sales.

Which combination of relationship properties should you modify to meet this requirement?

options.A: Change the cross-filter direction to **Both** and keep the relationship **Active**
options.B: Change the cross-filter direction to **Both** and set the relationship to **Inactive**
options.C: Keep the cross-filter direction as **Single** and change the relationship to **Inactive**
options.D: Change the relationship to **Inactive** and use a separate active relationship in the opposite direction

explanation:
**Statement 1 — Cross-filter Direction (Single vs Both): TRUE**

When a relationship is set to **Single** cross-filter direction, filtering flows in only one direction—typically from the "one" side (Product) to the "many" side (Sales). To enable bidirectional filtering, you must change the cross-filter direction to **Both**. This allows filters applied on either table to affect the other.

**Statement 2 — Active vs Inactive Status: TRUE**

An **Active** relationship is automatically used by Power BI in calculations and visualizations. An **Inactive** relationship requires explicit reference through the **USERELATIONSHIP()** function. Since the requirement is to have the relationship automatically apply the bidirectional filtering, the relationship must remain **Active**.

**Why other options are incorrect:**

- **Option B:** Setting the relationship to **Inactive** would require DAX functions to invoke it, which is unnecessary and defeats the purpose of automatic filtering.
- **Option C:** Keeping **Single** direction does not solve the problem; the filter will still only flow one direction.
- **Option D:** Creating a separate active relationship is not a standard approach and violates best practices for relationship design.

**Best Practice:** Use **Both** cross-filter direction sparingly, as it can lead to ambiguous filter contexts and performance degradation. However, when bidirectional filtering is required, **Both** with **Active** status is the correct configuration.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationship Properties – Cross-filter Direction (Single vs Both), Active vs Inactive

text:
You are designing a **Power BI data model** with three tables: **Sales**, **Products**, and **Regions**. The **Sales** table contains transaction records. You create a relationship between **Sales[ProductID]** and **Products[ProductID]**.

You need to filter visualizations so that when a user selects a product in a slicer, only sales data for that product appears across all visuals. However, you do **not** want the product selection to flow backward to filter the **Products** table itself.

Which combination of relationship properties should you configure?

options.A: **Active** relationship with **Single** cross-filter direction (from Products to Sales)
options.B: **Active** relationship with **Both** cross-filter direction
options.C: **Inactive** relationship with **Single** cross-filter direction (from Products to Sales)
options.D: **Inactive** relationship with **Both** cross-filter direction

explanation:
**Statement 1 — Active vs. Inactive: TRUE**

The relationship must be **Active** because you want automatic filtering to occur whenever a user interacts with a slicer or visual. An **inactive** relationship requires manual use of the **USERELATIONSHIP()** function in DAX and does not filter automatically.

**Statement 2 — Single vs. Both Cross-Filter Direction: TRUE**

You need **Single** cross-filter direction from **Products** → **Sales**. This means:
- When a product is selected, the filter *propagates forward* to **Sales** (the many-side table), filtering sales records.
- The filter does *not* propagate backward to the **Products** table.

If you chose **Both**, the product selection would also filter the **Products** table itself, limiting which products appear in the slicer—contrary to your requirement.

**Why not the others?**
- **Option B**: **Both** direction would cause bidirectional filtering, which is not the desired behavior.
- **Option C**: **Inactive** relationships do not filter automatically; explicit DAX functions would be needed.
- **Option D**: **Inactive** + **Both** combines two incorrect properties.

**Key Principle**: In Power BI, **Active** + **Single** direction is the standard for one-to-many relationships where you want predictable, automatic filtering in one direction only.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationship Properties – Cross-filter Direction (Single vs Both), Active vs Inactive

text:
You are building a **Power BI data model** for a retail analytics solution. Your model contains three tables:

- **Sales** (fact table) — contains transaction records with columns: SalesID, ProductID, StoreID, Amount, Date
- **Product** (dimension) — contains ProductID, ProductName, Category
- **Store** (dimension) — contains StoreID, StoreName, Region

Currently, both relationships (Sales→Product and Sales→Store) are configured with **Single** cross-filter direction and are **Active**.

A business requirement emerges: analysts need to create a report visual that filters sales by **Region** (from Store) and simultaneously shows the **total sales amount by Product Category** in the same slicer interaction. When a region is selected, the product category breakdown should reflect only sales from that region.

However, the current model configuration prevents this visualization from working as expected. The product totals are not being filtered by the selected region.

Which change to the relationship configuration would resolve this issue?

options.A: Change the Sales→Store relationship to **Both** cross-filter direction, keeping it Active
options.B: Change the Sales→Store relationship to Inactive, and create a new Active relationship with Both cross-filter direction
options.C: Change the Sales→Product relationship to **Both** cross-filter direction, keeping the Sales→Store relationship as Single
options.D: Create a new relationship between Product and Store with Both cross-filter direction, and deactivate the existing Sales relationships

explanation:
**Statement 1 — Why the current configuration fails — TRUE:**
With **Single** cross-filter direction on Sales→Store, filtering by Region (Store table) flows *into* Sales, but does **not** flow back *out* to Product. The Product table receives no filtering context from the Store selection, so product category totals remain unfiltered by region.

**Statement 2 — Understanding cross-filter direction — TRUE:**
- **Single direction**: Filtering flows one way (e.g., Store → Sales, but NOT onward to Product).
- **Both directions**: Filtering propagates across the relationship path (Store → Sales → Product and vice versa).

**Statement 3 — Why Option A is correct — TRUE:**
Changing Sales→Store to **Both** enables the filter chain: Store (Region selected) → Sales → Product. Now when Region is filtered, that context flows through Sales *to* Product, allowing product category totals to be dynamically filtered by the selected region. Keeping it **Active** ensures this is the default filter behavior without requiring DAX overrides.

**Statement 4 — Why other options fail — TRUE:**
- **Option B**: Deactivating and recreating relationships is unnecessary and would require explicit **USERELATIONSHIP()** DAX calls in measures — overcomplicates the solution.
- **Option C**: Changing Sales→Product to Both does not help; the problem is the lack of filter flow *from* Store *through* Sales *to* Product.
- **Option D**: Creating a direct Product↔Store relationship bypasses Sales and violates dimensional modeling best practices; relationships should flow through the fact table.

**Correct concept**: In a **star schema**, dimension-to-dimension filtering is achieved by configuring the fact-table relationships with **Both** cross-filter direction, enabling multi-dimensional slicing without breaking model integrity.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationship Properties – Cross-filter Direction (Single vs Both), Active vs Inactive

text:
You are building a **Power BI** data model for a retail company. The model contains three tables:

- **Sales** (fact table with SalesAmount, DateKey, CustomerKey, StoreKey)
- **Date** (dimension with DateKey — used as the active relationship to Sales)
- **Returns** (fact table with ReturnAmount, DateKey, CustomerKey)

Both **Sales** and **Returns** have a relationship to **Date** on DateKey. Because only one active relationship can exist between **Date** and the Sales/Returns tables, the relationship between **Date** and **Returns** is set as **inactive**.

A report page contains a slicer on **Date[Year]**. A card visual uses the following measure:

```
Total Returns = SUM(Returns[ReturnAmount])
```

A colleague reports that the **Date slicer does not filter the Total Returns card**. Which action will correctly resolve this issue *without* breaking the existing active relationship between **Date** and **Sales**?

options.A: Change the cross-filter direction on the inactive **Date** → **Returns** relationship from *Single* to *Both*.
options.B: Rewrite the measure using **CALCULATE** with **USERELATIONSHIP** to activate the **Date** → **Returns** relationship inside the measure context.
options.C: Delete the active relationship between **Date** and **Sales** and make the **Date** → **Returns** relationship active instead.
options.D: Set the cross-filter direction on the active **Date** → **Sales** relationship to *Both* so that filters propagate transitively to **Returns**.

explanation:
**Option B — CORRECT:** When two fact tables share the same dimension, only one relationship can be active at a time. The **USERELATIONSHIP** function inside **CALCULATE** temporarily activates a specified inactive relationship for the duration of that measure's evaluation, without changing or removing the active relationship used by other visuals. The corrected measure would be:

`Total Returns = CALCULATE(SUM(Returns[ReturnAmount]), USERELATIONSHIP(Date[DateKey], Returns[DateKey]))`

This allows the **Date** slicer to filter **Returns** correctly while leaving the active **Date** → **Sales** relationship intact.

**Option A — INCORRECT:** Changing the *cross-filter direction* to *Both* affects how filters propagate between tables that are already connected by an **active** relationship. An inactive relationship does not participate in filter propagation regardless of its cross-filter direction setting; the relationship must be activated (via **USERELATIONSHIP**) for it to have any effect.

**Option C — INCORRECT:** Deleting the active **Date** → **Sales** relationship would break date filtering on all Sales-related visuals, which is unacceptable.

**Option D — INCORRECT:** Setting the active **Date** → **Sales** relationship to *Both* cross-filter direction enables bidirectional filtering between **Date** and **Sales**, but it does not cause filters to propagate to **Returns**. **Returns** is a separate fact table with its own (inactive) relationship to **Date**; bidirectional filtering on the Sales path has no bearing on the Returns path.

metadata.subject: Model the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Relationship Properties – Cross-filter Direction (Single vs Both), Active vs Inactive

text:
